column15: description_truncated

Acre was the Crusaders' greatest port. Like Jerusalem, it fell to Saladin in 1187; but the Third Crusade recovered it in 1191, and from then until 1291 it served as the de facto capital of the Kingdom of Jerusalem — exactly a hundred years. The siege that restored it, begun by Guy of Lusignan in August 1189 and completed by Richard the Lionheart and Philip II of France in July 1191, was the longest of the Crusades. A quarrel over banners nearly broke the crusade in half at its end: Leopold V of ...

column4: al-Quds; Hierusalem; Yerushalayim; Royal Domain of Jerusalem
column15: Jerusalem was the spiritual and political heart of the Crusader Kingdom from its bloody capture by Godfrey of Bouillon's army on 15 July 1099 until its surrender to Saladin in early October 1187, three months after the Frankish defeat at the Horns of Hattin on 4 July. The garrison, swollen by refugees, held out for about twelve days before Balian of Ibelin negotiated the city's surrender. Godfrey refused the title of king, styling himself Advocatus Sancti Sepulchri — Defender of the Holy Sepulch...

column4: Chastelet; le Chastelez; Vadum Iacob; Mezad Ateret; Ateret Fortress
column15: The Castle of Jacob's Ford — Chastelet or Vadum Iacob — was a brand-new castle that King Baldwin IV began in 1178 on a mound beside the Jordan, at the point where the river could be forded on the road from Galilee to Damascus. Saladin attacked it in August 1179 before it was finished, and destroyed it after a short siege; hundreds of Templars were killed or captured. Israeli excavations have uncovered the unfinished fortifications and the bodies of the defenders exactly where they fell. The site...

column4: Athlith; Pilgrims' Castle; Castrum Peregrinorum; Chastiau Pelerin; Castrum Filit Det
column15: Atlit — the Castle of the Pilgrims (Château Pèlerin) — was built by the Templars from 1217 on a narrow sea-girt promontory south of Haifa, and became one of the largest and most sophisticated Crusader castles ever constructed. Its double landward walls, sea-level rock-cut ditch and massive inner towers made it essentially impregnable: it was the last Frankish stronghold on the Levantine mainland, evacuated only in August 1291, three months after Acre had already fallen. The site is now a closed ...

column4: Bethgibelin; Beit Gibelin; Bersabea; Gybelin; Ybelin Hospitalariorum; Bet Guvrin; Eleutheropolis
column15: Known to the Crusaders as Bethgibelin, this was one of four castles built around Ascalon in the 1130s to strangle the Fatimid garrison there. Granted to the Knights Hospitaller in 1136, it was a concentric fortress with a 50-metre-square inner ward and projecting corner towers. The castle fell to Saladin after Hattin in 1187 and was permanently lost to the Franks. Its ruins are preserved in Beit Guvrin-Maresha National Park, alongside Byzantine churches and vast bell-shaped caves.

Belmont Castle was a Hospitaller fortress built in the 1160s on a hill west of Jerusalem, dominating the approach roads from the coastal plain. It had a rectangular inner ward, a polygonal outer ward, stables, a wine-press, cisterns and a vaulted passage down to its spring. Lost to Saladin after Hattin and never recovered by the Franks, its extensive ruins are today in a forested area above Kibbutz Tzuba.

Belvoir — Kokhav HaYarden in Hebrew — is the best-preserved Crusader concentric castle in Israel. Perched 500 metres above the Jordan Valley, the Hospitaller fortress withstood an eighteen-month Ayyubid siege after the disaster at Hattin in 1187 and only surrendered on honourable terms in 1189, when the defenders were allowed to march out with their arms. Its massive walls, dry moat, inner and outer wards, and rounded corner towers are today the centrepiece of an Israeli national park.

column4: Qalaat al-Tantura; Qul'at al-Tantura; Titora Hill
column15: A small castle or road fortress above the Modi'in plain, known in Arabic as Qal'at al-Tantura and in Hebrew as Tel Titora. It likely guarded the road from the coastal plain up to Jerusalem. The masonry remains are modest but occupy a prominent hill now in the middle of modern Modi'in-Maccabim-Re'ut.

The Frankish castle of Abelina stood on a hill above the modern Galilean town of Ibillin. It consisted of a central tower with a twin barrel-vaulted basement inside an enclosing wall; the rounded outer towers visible today are mostly later additions. The lordship of Abelina belonged to the influential Ibelin family for much of the twelfth century.

column4: Zir'in; Gezrael; Jezrael; Parvum Gerinum; Zaratn; Tel Yizra'el
column15: Tel Yizre'el overlooks the Jezreel Valley from a strategic ridge and had a small Crusader castle that belonged to the Templars by the 1180s. The valley was one of the main east-west corridors of the kingdom and was the route Saladin's army took in the summer of 1187 toward the disaster at Hattin.

column4: Toron des Chevaliers/de los Caballeros; Toronum Militum; al-Natrun
column15: Built by the Templars in the mid-twelfth century as 'Toron des Chevaliers', Latrun guarded the main road from the coastal plain to Jerusalem. A large quadrangular castle with a talus, portcullised gate, outer ward and stables, it was lost to Saladin after Hattin in 1187 and only briefly reoccupied during the Third Crusade before passing permanently out of Frankish hands a few years later. The medieval ruins share the hill with a nineteenth-century Trappist monastery.

Mirabel was the castle of the Ibelin family, one of the most influential dynasties of the Crusader kingdom. Built around an early twelfth-century square tower, it grew into a more complex fortification on the edge of the Samarian hills. A Turkish fortress and village later reused much of the medieval fabric; today the site is within Migdal Afek National Park east of Tel Aviv.

column4: Mhalia; Castrum Regis; Chastiau dou Ret; Castellium Novum; Castellum Novum Regis; Ma'alot
column15: The royal castle of Castellum Novum Regis ('the King's New Castle') was built on a hill above the Galilean village of Mi'ilya around the middle of the twelfth century. It later passed to the Teutonic Knights. Remnants of the hilltop enceinte and corner towers are still visible among the houses of today's village, which has one of the largest Melkite Christian populations in Israel.

Montfort — 'the strong mountain' — was the headquarters of the Teutonic Order in the Holy Land from the 1220s. Perched on a narrow spur deep in Upper Galilee, with a D-shaped donjon, great hall, and multi-level vaulted ranges, it was built specifically to house the order's treasury and archives. The Mamluk sultan Baybars besieged and took it in 1271 after tunnelling under its walls. Today its dramatic ruins, reached on foot from Nahal Kziv, are one of the most picturesque Crusader sites in Israe...

column4: Castellum Novum; Chastel Neuf; Mezudat Hunin; Horvat Mezudat Hunin
column15: Chastel Neuf ('New Castle') overlooked the upper Jordan from a ridge on the modern Israel-Lebanon border. Repeatedly fought over during the twelfth century, it was destroyed and rebuilt several times before finally being lost in 1266. The conspicuous standing masonry that dominates the Margaliot lookout today is largely Ottoman, but the Crusader moat and earlier walls can still be traced beneath it.

Yehi'am Fortress in western Galilee grew from a small Crusader castle into a larger Ottoman stronghold, and was reused again by the defenders of Kibbutz Yehi'am during the 1948 war. The surviving twelfth-century inner ward walls are now the centrepiece of an Israeli national park.

Qaqun was a Templar castle on a low mound commanding the Via Maris between Caesarea and Megiddo. Its two-storey tower, some 17 metres across with walls almost 3 metres thick, survived much of the later medieval period; the Mamluks defeated a Mongol raid here in 1271. The tower still stands to about 8 metres in height on a hill east of the modern village of Qaqun.

The Templar castle of Safed, on a steep hill above the old upper-Galilee town of the same name, was one of the largest and most ambitious Crusader fortifications ever built. Reconstructed on a grand scale between 1240 and 1260 by the Templars, it had an oval concentric plan measuring roughly 120 by 280 metres. Baybars captured it in 1266 after a long siege and the surrender terms were infamously broken: the defenders who had been promised safe passage were executed en masse. Only fragments of it...

column4: S. Margareta cast(ellum); Cava Templi (castrum); Mount Carmel
column15: A Templar castle on the headland of Mount Carmel above modern Haifa, dedicated to Saint Margaret. Destroyed in 1821, its foundations now support the Stella Maris lighthouse. The site had been a Frankish outpost as early as the twelfth century, watching the northern approach to the port of Acre.

column4: al-BURJ; Qal'at Tantura, al-Habis; Gith, Git, Tarenta (-), Tharenta (2); TANTURA; Merle; Dor
column15: The castle of Merle occupied a sea-girt promontory at the southern end of Dor Bay. Defended by the sea on three sides and a deep ditch on the fourth, it was a classic coastal Frankish strongpoint. A corner tower survived until the 1890s, when it finally collapsed. Today the site is within the Dor-HaBonim Beach Nature Reserve.

column4: Caimun; Caun Mons; Caymont; Mons Cain; al-Qaymun; Tel Yoqne'am
column15: Tel Yokne'am guards the entrance to the Jezreel Valley through a narrow pass in the Carmel range. A Crusader castle known as Caymont, with houses, a tower, a donjon, and a church inside a defensive wall, occupied the mound in the twelfth century. It was at Caymont that the rump of the Crusader army regrouped after the disaster at Hattin.

column4: Blanchegarde; Blanca Guarda; Tall as-Safiya; Tell es-Safi; Tel Tzafit
column15: Blanchegarde — 'the white watchtower' — was one of the ring of castles built around Ascalon in the 1140s, this one founded by King Fulk in 1142 on the chalky hilltop that gave it its name. A square quadriburgium with four corner towers, it fell to Saladin after Hattin in 1187. Today Tel Tzafit is best known for large excavations of Bronze and Iron Age Philistine layers, but the Crusader fort is still visible on top of the mound.

Yavne — known to the Crusaders as Ibelin — was one of the four castles thrown up around Ascalon in the 1140s and became the seat of the powerful Ibelin family. William of Tyre called the twelfth-century castle 'very strongly built with deep foundations and four towers'. The original quadriburgium is mostly gone, but its hilltop in the centre of modern Yavne still preserves traces of the medieval fortification.

column4: Casellum de Planis; Case! des Plains; Casale/Casellum Balneorum; Casellum de Templo
column15: The Crusader manor of Casellum de Planis stood at the modern town of Azor, just south-east of Tel Aviv and Jaffa. Its square central tower, with barrel-vaulted basement and a stair rising through the vault, formed the core of a small lordship of the Jaffa county.

column4: at-Taiyiba; 'Afra; Castle of St Elias; Saint Elyes; Effraon; castrum S. Helyae
column15: A small Frankish castle perched above the modern village of Tuba Zangariyya / Taibe in eastern Galilee, known in the sources as the Castle of St Elias. It guarded the upland road between the Sea of Galilee and the Jezreel Valley. Part of its concentric walls, a barrel-vaulted gate passage, and remains of a square inner tower still stand among the village houses.

column4: Akhziv; Casel Imbert; Casale Huberti de Paci; Casale Lamberti; Castellum Ziph; Qasale Imbert/Siph
column15: Achziv, on the Mediterranean coast north of Acre, was the Crusader 'new town' of Casal Imbert, belonging to the royal domain of Acre. It was the site of a sharp Frankish defeat by Saladin's troops in 1187 and later briefly held by the Teutonic Knights. A tower once stood in the village; traces of Crusader occupation are mixed with much earlier Phoenician and later Ottoman remains, today inside Achziv National Park.

Arsuf — ancient Apollonia — was a small but strategically placed port on the sandstone cliffs north of modern Tel Aviv. The Crusaders took it in 1101, and in 1191 its coastal plain hosted the Battle of Arsuf, one of Richard the Lionheart's most famous victories, when the Crusader army broke Saladin's harassing cavalry in a disciplined charge. The town remained in Frankish hands until 1265, when the Mamluk sultan Baybars besieged and destroyed it. The concentric castle with its rounded towers, ha...

Ascalon was the last Fatimid foothold on the Palestinian coast and for fifty years after the First Crusade it remained a thorn in the kingdom's side — raiding parties from Ascalon reached the gates of Jerusalem. King Baldwin III finally captured it in 1153 after a long siege. Saladin retook it in 1187; Richard the Lionheart refortified it in 1192 with at least fourteen towers. Today a great horseshoe of Crusader and earlier walls survives in Tel Ashkelon National Park.

Caesarea Maritima, the great Roman and Byzantine port, became one of the most heavily fortified Crusader cities after Baldwin I took it in 1101. The walls visible today — a near-complete circuit of curtain, towers and a dry moat — were built by King Louis IX of France in 1251-2 during his stay in the Holy Land. The city, its harbour, and its Frankish cathedral survived until the Mamluks systematically demolished the coastal defences in 1265. Caesarea is now one of Israel's most visited archaeolo...

column4: Joppa, Yafa; Jaffa, Japhe, Joppe; Yafo
column15: Jaffa was the main port of entry for pilgrims and Crusaders throughout the twelfth and thirteenth centuries. It changed hands repeatedly: Richard the Lionheart defended it personally against Saladin in 1192, famously wading ashore at the head of a small force. Frederick II rebuilt its walls in 1228, and Louis IX strengthened them again in 1252. Little of the medieval city survives above ground, but its walled citadel hill and the faubourg below it shaped the topography of modern Old Jaffa.

column4: Jerusalem; Ierosolima; Yerushalayim; David's Tower; Tower of David; al-Quds ash-Sharif
column15: The Citadel next to the Jaffa Gate was the residence of the Crusader kings of Jerusalem from 1099 until 1187 and again briefly from 1229 to 1244. Built around a gigantic Herodian-period tower known today as the Tower of David, the Crusader castle straddled the city wall and commanded the western approach to Jerusalem. Much of its medieval masonry, gatehouses and moats is still visible inside the Tower of David Museum.

column7: Old City, Jerusalem (NW corner of city wall)
column15: A massive polygonal tower at the northwest corner of Jerusalem's walls, built originally on Fatimid foundations and stormed by Tancred of Antioch in the 1099 siege — the episode from which the Crusaders took the tower's new name. Under Frankish rule it served as the principal anchor of the northern defences of the royal city, linked to the Citadel and the Tower of David. The Ayyubids partly dismantled it after 1219; Mamluks and Ottomans rebuilt over the ruins. Today only foundations survive, vis...

Tiberias was the capital of the Principality of Galilee and one of the great feudal seats of the Crusader kingdom. Count Raymond III of Tripoli held the city through his wife Eschiva, and it was the siege of Tiberias by Saladin in July 1187 that drew the Frankish army out to its destruction at the Horns of Hattin a few days later. Fragmentary remains of the medieval castle, city walls and a lakeside tower survive along the modern waterfront.

A beautifully preserved Crusader courtyard building in a spring-fed glade west of Jerusalem, now the heart of Ein Hemed National Park. It was most likely built as a Hospitaller estate and infirmary in the twelfth century, and its thick walls, arrow-slits, and vaulted halls made it easy to defend even though its primary function was agricultural. It is one of the best-surviving examples of a Crusader 'maison forte' (fortified manor) in the region.

A striking fortified hall-house on a ridge in the Judean hills west of Jerusalem, built in the twelfth century as a small Frankish manor. A slit machicolation and a draw-bar protected the ground-floor entrance while vaulted rooms and a central courtyard served the lord's household. The ruins still crown the hilltop beside the modern village of Nes Harim.

A massive twelfth-century tower once stood beside the old Jerusalem road at Beit Safafa, today a neighbourhood in southern Jerusalem. Its barrel-vaulted basement, cistern, internal stair, and upper floors housed a small Frankish garrison whose job was to keep the road to the capital safe. Only fragmentary remains survive.

column4: Se Cr. Bethsan, Bessan, Betsan; Bet She'an
column15: Beit She'an, one of the oldest continuously inhabited sites in the region, was a Frankish administrative centre in the twelfth century. A massive square tower some 17 metres across, built partly of reused Roman ashlar blocks, stood a few hundred metres from the great Roman theatre. It was incorporated into the later Ottoman 'Old Serail' and its Crusader core can still be traced in the old town.

A very large square tower at Horvat Mezad Abbirim in Upper Galilee. Its exact date is debated — parts of it may actually be pre-Crusader — but it stood at the edge of the Frankish zone and was probably reused during the twelfth century as a watchtower.

column4: Khirbat al-Burj al-'Atut; The Red Tower; Turris Latinae; Turriclee; Tourre-Rouge; Turris Rubea; H. Burgata
column15: The 'Red Tower' was a Frankish hall-tower controlling a stretch of the coastal plain near modern Burgata. Rebuilt in stone in the twelfth century, it was held by the Templars, the Hospitallers, and at various times by secular lords. A tall standing wall and the foundations of a barrel-vaulted base still mark the site.

column4: Tall al-Malat; al-Malaha; al-Mallitha; Turris Salinarum; Tel Tanninim
column15: Tel Tanninim — 'the mound of the crocodiles' — lies just north of Caesarea at the mouth of the Nahal Taninim stream. A small shore tower and an oval defensive enclosure with gates, cisterns, and vaults were maintained here, linked in the sources to the Hospitallers. Much of what survives is older Byzantine and Roman harbour fabric reused by the Franks.

At the foot of Mount Tabor, Dabburiya was a small fortified village on the Tiberias road. A medieval tower, still visible incorporated into the modern mosque, probably preserves part of a Crusader strongpoint undermined during Saladin's campaign of 1182.

column4: e-em Kafr Lab; Cafarlet, Capharleth, Kafarletum, Hebr
column15: An early Islamic fort of trapezoidal plan on the Mediterranean coast, reused by the Crusaders as a small fortified estate. Its four solid cylindrical corner turrets and external buttresses are strikingly well-preserved and date mostly to the Abbasid period, but the building remained in use right through the Frankish twelfth century.

column4: Casale Latinae; casale quod fuit Eustachii; Montdidier; Mondisder; Mons Dederi; Kibbutz Ma'barot; Hafita
column15: A Frankish village estate linked to the abbey of St Mary Latin in Jerusalem and later to the Hospitallers and Templars. A tower with a twin barrel-vaulted basement once stood at the site, which today lies within the fields of Kibbutz Ma'abarot in the Sharon plain.

column4: Kh. Baubriya; Manueth; H. Manot; Horbat Manot
column15: A Crusader-period sugar-production complex with an attached courtyard building and tower, acquired by the Hospitallers in the thirteenth century. Sugar was one of the kingdom's most profitable industries, and sites like Manawat were essentially fortified agricultural factories run by the military orders. The ruins are now in Horbat Manot in western Galilee.

column4: Francheville (2); Riishmiya; H. Roshi Maya; Rosh Maya; Haifa-Romena
column15: A rectangular Frankish tower, probably a small manor, on the western slope of Mount Carmel above Haifa. Known in the sources as 'Francheville', a name that hints at a privileged Frankish settler community. Substantial basement and wall remains survive today in the Romema neighbourhood of Haifa.

Kurdani — known as Doc or Recordane in the Crusader sources — was an important milling and sugar-production complex on the Na'aman River a few kilometres east of Acre. Water-powered mills ground flour and processed sugar cane for the kingdom's economy. The site was also the scene of a sharp engagement during Saladin's campaign around Acre in 1189. Substantial remains of dams, water channels, and vaulted mill buildings survive today in the Ein Afek Nature Reserve.

column4: Casel Destretz; le Destrott; Destrictium; Petra Inctsa; Le Destroit; H. Qarta
column15: Le Destroit — 'the narrow place' — was a small Templar road-station castle just south of Atlit, guarding the coast road where it squeezes between the sea and a sandstone ridge. Built in the twelfth century, it included rock-cut stables, reservoirs and a tower on a battered base. It was deliberately slighted in 1220 when the Templars moved their operations to the much larger castle at Atlit.

column4: Castellum Beroart; Castel Brouar; Mahiz Azdiid; H. Ashdod Yam; Ashdod-Yam
column15: Ashdod-Yam ('Ashdod-on-the-Sea') began life as an Abbasid coastal fort with solid rounded corner turrets, and was reoccupied and modestly rebuilt by the Crusaders as a small defended anchorage. The well-preserved walls and turrets are now a public heritage site on the Ashdod beach.

column4: Berniki Hill; Mount Berniki; Har Berenice
column15: Mount Berenice — the steep hill immediately above the hot springs at the south end of Tiberias — has been fortified since antiquity. In the Crusader period a small fortification occupied the summit, commanding views over the Sea of Galilee and the Jordan Valley approaches. The hill is named after the Herodian princess Berenice, and archaeological work has uncovered Roman, Byzantine, and Crusader-period remains including a church and ancillary structures. The site is now a partially excavated arc...

A Hospitaller estate in the coastal plain acquired in 1181 and devoted mainly to sugar production. The main surviving building is the barrel-vaulted base of a tower some 17 metres across, with an attached long vaulted structure that probably formed part of the sugar factory.

A Hospitaller estate centre in the coastal plain. The medieval complex in the village centre included a tower, a substantial first-floor hall over a vaulted basement, and additional vaulted ranges around a cistern. The Hospitallers held the village until it was lost to the Mamluks around 1265.
A Hospitaller village estate producing revenue for the order from 1166 onwards, with a vaulted building inside an outer wall. The site is today partly overlain by modern Rantiya in central Israel.

Saffuriya — ancient Sepphoris — had a heavy square Crusader tower built of reused Roman masonry, including sarcophagus blocks. The site is famous for a different reason: it is where the Crusader army mustered under Guy of Lusignan in July 1187 before marching east to catastrophic defeat at the Horns of Hattin. The tower, much modified in the Ottoman period, still stands within Tzipori National Park. A Crusader-period church dedicated to St Anne, believed to mark the home of Mary's parents Joachi...

column4: Summil; Summayl; Khirbet Sumail; סומייל אל-ח'ליל; Bir Summail
column15: A Crusader castle built by the Hospitallers in 1168 to protect the fortress at Bayt Jibrin. Pringle records remains of a medieval castle in the village, consisting of a central tower enclosed by a roughly square enceinte (over 30 m east–west) with talus and square corner towers. The principal surviving element is the north enclosure wall, which stands to a height of over 8 m and is over 30 m long and 1.5 m thick. A medieval masonry well (Bir Summail) lies to the south. The village was depopulate...

Only the basement of a small Crusader tower survives on Tel Hannaton, a mound in the Lower Galilee. Modest in scale, the tower was part of the network of rural Frankish strongpoints that dotted the Galilean countryside in the twelfth century.

Medieval tower on a rocky islet at the end of the southern breakwater of the port of Acre, forming the seaward anchor of the harbour chain that closed off the Crusader harbour. First documented in the 12th century and named for a pre-Crusader shrine (Baal-zebub, 'lord of flies'). Destroyed and rebuilt repeatedly; submerged foundations survive and are visible above the waterline at low tide.

column4: Mukhalid; Castellare Rogerii Longobardi; Castellum Rogerii Langobardi; Nathanya; Netanya
column15: A Frankish courtyard building at the site of modern Netanya's old core. Documented in 1135, it was a small manor with barrel-vaulted ranges and a projecting turret at one corner. Most of the structure was destroyed in 1948 and only fragmentary walls survive in the Umm Khalid neighbourhood.

A fortified tower roughly 19 metres square once stood on the low rise at the centre of modern Afula, guarding the fertile Jezreel Valley between the hills of Galilee and Samaria. The tower belonged to the network of small Frankish strongpoints that policed the valley roads rather than any single great lordship.

column4: Basilica of the Annunciation; Nazareth Cathedral
column15: Nazareth fell to Tancred in 1102 and was elevated to an archbishopric, and a first Crusader church was built over the Byzantine ruins above the traditional grotto of the Annunciation. Under Archbishop Letard II in the 1170s the Franks began an ambitious second basilica that aimed to rival anything being built in contemporary France. Work stopped abruptly when the kingdom collapsed at Hattin in 1187. Excavations in 1909 uncovered five carved Romanesque capitals — never installed — depicting scene...

column4: Cathedral of St. John the Baptist; Great Mosque of Ramla; al-Jami' al-Kabir
column15: The Crusader Cathedral of St. John the Baptist was the principal Latin church of Ramla, built in the mid-twelfth century as a three-aisled basilica with pointed arches and cross-vaults in the mature Crusader Romanesque style. After the Muslim reconquest the building was converted into the Great Mosque of Ramla, a function it still serves today. Substantial elements of the Crusader structure — the nave arcades, the vaults, and carved capitals — remain visible inside the mosque.

column2: Cathedral of the Holy Cross, Acre
column3: Cathedral of the Holy Cross, Acre
column7: Old City of Acre, near El-Jazzar Mosque
column15: After Saladin took Jerusalem in 1187 the Latin Patriarchate moved north to Acre, and the Cathedral of the Holy Cross — rebuilt around the recovered city after 1191 — became the principal cathedral of what historians call the 'Second Kingdom of Jerusalem'. The cathedral stood next to the great Hospitaller commandery of Saint-Jean-d'Acre and was the seat of the Latin Patriarch from 1191 until the city's fall. When Sultan al-Ashraf Khalil's Mamluks took Acre in 1291 the cathedral was systematically...

Rebuilt by the Crusaders around 1150 on the summit of the Mount of Olives at the traditional site of Christ's ascension. Saladin's armies took the hill in 1187 and converted the complex into a mosque, but the Crusader-built octagonal shrine (the edicule) survives intact — it still houses the 'Ascension Rock' and today serves as an Islamic shrine shared for Christian pilgrimage.

Grand 12th-century Crusader church over the symbolic tomb of Saint George at Lydda, a major pilgrimage site and the seat of the Bishop of Lydda. Razed by Saladin's armies in 1191; a Mamluk mosque was raised over the ruins in 1268, and a Greek Orthodox church was rebuilt on the eastern end of the Crusader nave and northern aisle in the 19th century, sharing the site with the mosque.

column2: Church of Saint John the Baptist, Ein Karem
column3: Church of Saint John the Baptist, Ein Karem
column4: Ein Karem Church of St John; Birthplace of John the Baptist
column15: Tradition identified the village of Ein Karem in the Judean hills west of Jerusalem as the birthplace of John the Baptist. Russian abbot Daniel, who visited the Holy Land in 1106, described a 'small cavern' there where John was born. The Crusaders built a vaulted three-apse Romanesque church directly over the grotto in the twelfth century. After 1187 the building was abandoned by the Christian community and used as a stable; in the seventeenth century the Franciscans regained the site and rebuil...

column2: Church of Saint Mary of the Germans (Jerusalem)
column3: Church of Saint Mary of the Germans (Jerusalem)
column7: Church of Saint Mary of the Germans (Jerusalem)
column15: Romanesque church built in 1128 in the Jewish Quarter of Crusader Jerusalem as part of a larger German pilgrimage complex that included a hospital and hostel for German-speaking pilgrims. Partly destroyed in the 13th century during the Ayyubid recapture of Jerusalem; its ruins were rediscovered in 1968 during post-1967 archaeological work and are now preserved as an open-air monument.

column4: Church of the Resurrection; Basilica of the Holy Sepulchre; Ecclesia Sancti Sepulchri
column7: Christian Quarter, Old City of Jerusalem
column15: The Church of the Holy Sepulchre — the holiest site in Christianity — was the spiritual heart of the Crusader kingdom. The church standing today is largely the Crusader building consecrated on 15 July 1149, the fiftieth anniversary of the capture of Jerusalem. The Crusaders unified the separate Byzantine chapels into a single Romanesque cathedral with a grand entrance facade, a bell tower, a dome over the rotunda, and a choir above the crypt of St Helena. It was here that the kings of Jerusalem ...

Three-apsed Romanesque church built in twelfth-century Crusader Caesarea. Louis IX of France, who spent four years in the Holy Land after his defeat in Egypt, heavily refortified the town's walls in 1251–1252. In 1265 the town and church fell to the Mamluk sultan Baibars, who razed the city so thoroughly — tearing down its harbour, walls and churches — that no Frankish re-occupation would ever be possible. The church's apsidal foundations, partly excavated, still stand within the Israeli nationa...

column4: Church of the Resurrection; Hospitaller Church of Abu Ghosh; Kiryat Ye'arim Church
column15: The Crusader church at Abu Ghosh was built around 1141 by the Knights Hospitaller over a natural spring, which feeds a cistern in the crypt. The village was identified in the twelfth century with the biblical Emmaus, the place where the risen Christ appeared to two disciples. The church preserves remarkable twelfth-century frescoes in the apse and crypt, making it one of the most important surviving examples of Crusader religious art. It is now maintained by French Benedictine monks and is open ...

Emmaus Nicopolis, in the Ayalon Valley near Latrun, was one of the traditional sites for the New Testament Emmaus. The Crusaders built a church here in the twelfth century on the ruins of a large Byzantine basilica, reusing Roman and earlier masonry. The remains of both the Crusader and Byzantine churches are preserved in an open-air archaeological park run by a small community near Canada Park.

column2: Hospitaller Church of Saint John, Acre
column3: Hospitaller Church of Saint John, Acre
column7: Old City of Acre, beneath the Citadel
column15: The Knights Hospitaller's principal commandery in Acre — the international headquarters of the order from 1191 until the city's fall in 1291 — included a three-aisled Romanesque church of Saint John the Baptist, a vast vaulted refectory, a knights' hall, infirmary wards, and a hospital that could accommodate more than a thousand beds. Excavations beneath the Ottoman citadel have uncovered six parallel vaulted halls with early-Gothic ribs, the dining hall with its marble columns, the underground ...

column4: Church of Saint Anne; Basilica of Saint Anne; Kanisat Sitt Hanne
column7: Near Lion's Gate, Old City of Jerusalem
column15: St. Anne's Church is one of the best-preserved Crusader churches in the entire Holy Land. Built in the 1140s near the Pools of Bethesda and the Lion's Gate, it is a masterpiece of austere Romanesque architecture with superb acoustics. Tradition holds it as the birthplace of the Virgin Mary. After Saladin captured Jerusalem in 1187 he converted the church into a madrasa — the inscription above the door still reads — and it fell into disrepair until the Ottoman sultan gave it to France in 1856. To...

column4: Dome of the Rock; Qubbat al-Sakhra; Temple of the Lord
column7: Temple Mount, Old City of Jerusalem
column15: The seventh-century Umayyad Dome of the Rock was the most spectacular building in Jerusalem when the Crusaders captured the city in 1099. Believing — wrongly — that the structure was a remnant of the Temple of Solomon, the Franks Christianised it without demolishing it. They placed a great gilded cross atop the dome, fixed an altar to St Nicholas on the bare bedrock of the Foundation Stone (1162), and surrounded the rock with a decorative iron grille to prevent pilgrims from chipping it for reli...

column4: Templum Salomonis; Al-Aqsa Mosque (Templar HQ); Solomon's Temple
column7: Temple Mount, Old City of Jerusalem
column15: The Crusaders identified the Al-Aqsa Mosque on the south end of the Temple Mount as the Templum Salomonis — Solomon's royal palace — and used it as the residence of the first two kings of Jerusalem, Baldwin I and Baldwin II. In 1118 Baldwin II moved the royal household to the Citadel by the Tower of David and granted the mosque to a small group of knights who had vowed to protect pilgrims on the road from Jaffa to Jerusalem. The new order took its name — the Knights of the Temple of Solomon, or ...

column4: Church of the Sepulchre of Saint Mary; Tomb of Mary
column7: Kidron Valley, Mount of Olives, Jerusalem
column15: Cut partly into the bedrock at the foot of the Mount of Olives, the Crusader Church of the Sepulchre of Saint Mary marks the traditional tomb of the Virgin. The Frankish builders in the twelfth century reshaped a fifth-century Byzantine martyrium into a cruciform church with a wide forty-eight-step descending staircase that survives almost intact today. Queen Melisende of Jerusalem was buried here in 1161 in a side chapel halfway down the stairs, and the church became the principal funerary site...

On 3 September 1260, the Mamluk sultan Qutuz and his commander Baybars met the Mongol army of Kitbuqa at the Spring of Goliath (Ayn Jalut) in the Jezreel Valley, a few hours' march from Crusader Acre. The Crusader barons in Acre had declared a calculated neutrality and granted the Mamluks safe passage and a market through their territory — a tacit acknowledgement that the Mongol threat outweighed the Muslim one. Baybars led the Mamluk vanguard in a feigned retreat that drew the Mongols into an a...

column7: Al-Sinnabra bridge, south of the Sea of Galilee
column15: On 28 June 1113, a Seljuk army from Mosul and Damascus under Mawdud and Toghtekin caught Baldwin I's forces by surprise near the al-Sinnabra bridge at the southern end of the Sea of Galilee. The Seljuks lured the Franks across the bridge with a feigned retreat — a textbook Turkish tactic — and then enveloped them. The Crusaders lost 1,000 to 2,000 men, including 30 knights, and Baldwin barely escaped, leaving his royal banner and tent in Seljuk hands. Recognising that he could not afford a secon...

A masterclass in Crusader battlefield discipline. After recapturing Acre in July 1191, Richard the Lionheart began a cautious march south along the coast toward Jaffa, keeping the sea on his right and his army in tight formation. Saladin shadowed him inland, sending waves of mounted archers to provoke the Crusader cavalry into a premature charge. For days Richard's knights endured a rain of arrows without breaking ranks — an extraordinary feat of discipline. On 7 September, near the ruins of Ars...

Barely a month after taking Jerusalem, the Crusaders faced their first real counter-attack. The Fatimid vizier al-Afdal had assembled a large Egyptian relief army — chroniclers claim twenty thousand or more — and marched it up the coast to Ascalon to retake the Holy City. The Crusader leadership — Godfrey of Bouillon together with the two Roberts (of Normandy and Flanders) and Raymond of Toulouse — rode south with roughly twelve hundred knights and perhaps nine thousand infantry to meet the thre...

On 15 July 1182, Baldwin IV — by now visibly disabled by leprosy and unable to ride without assistance — repulsed a major Saladin invasion at Belvoir Castle, on the bluff above the Jordan Valley. Commanding 700 knights and 9,000 infantry and Turcopoles, the king refused to be drawn into the open and held a tight defensive formation at the foot of the castle. The Ayyubid army inflicted heavy casualties on the Frankish infantry but could not break the knights, and after a day of inconclusive fight...

column7: Springs of Cresson, near Saffuriya, Lower Galilee
column15: On 1 May 1187, two months before the catastrophe at Hattin, a small Templar and Hospitaller force led by the Templar grand master Gerard of Ridefort intercepted a far larger Ayyubid raiding column near the Springs of Cresson, just outside Nazareth. Despite repeated warnings that the enemy outnumbered them ten to one, Gerard ordered an immediate charge. The result was a slaughter: the Hospitaller master Roger des Moulins was killed, and only Gerard and three knights escaped. The destruction of ne...

column7: Horns of Hattin, Lower Galilee, Israel
column15: The single most catastrophic day in Crusader history. By mid-1187 Saladin had united Egypt and Syria and assembled the largest Muslim army the Crusaders had ever faced. King Guy of Jerusalem, goaded by aggressive barons, marched his entire field army — some twenty thousand men, virtually every knight in the kingdom — away from the springs of Saffuriya on a waterless July march toward Tiberias. Saladin's cavalry harassed them relentlessly through the scorching heat. On the morning of 4 July the e...

The last major clash of the Third Crusade and perhaps Richard the Lionheart's most astonishing personal feat of arms. In late July 1192 Saladin launched a surprise assault on Jaffa while Richard was negotiating at Acre. The city's outer defences fell, and the small garrison retreated into the citadel. Richard raced south by galley with only a handful of knights and a few hundred Italian crossbowmen. He waded ashore and, with barely seventeen mounted knights and a thin line of infantry, counter-a...

One of the most dramatic upsets in Crusader history. In November 1177 Saladin led a large raiding force — perhaps twenty-six thousand men — across the frontier from Egypt, pushing deep into the kingdom while its army was scattered. The sixteen-year-old King Baldwin IV, already suffering from leprosy, gathered what forces he could find: barely 375 knights, a company of Templars from Gaza, and a few thousand infantry. Carrying the relic of the True Cross, this small force intercepted Saladin near ...

column4: Battle of Ibelin; Battle of Yibna
column15: With Baldwin II in captivity in Aleppo, the regent Eustace Grenier led a Crusader field army of 7,000 men against a Fatimid invasion force of 16,000 sent by the vizier Al-Ma'mun al-Bata'ihi. They met on 29 May 1123 between Ascalon and Jaffa, near Yibna (Yavne). The Frankish charge shattered the Egyptian line, killing some 6,000 Fatimid soldiers. The following day, 30 May, the Venetian fleet under Doge Domenico Michiel destroyed the Fatimid navy off the coast of Jaffa. The twin victories effectiv...

Between 30 September and 6 October 1183, the regent Guy of Lusignan — newly raised to the regency in place of the ailing Baldwin IV — confronted a major Saladin invasion of the Jezreel Valley. Guy mobilised the entire field army of the kingdom, more than 15,000 infantry and 1,300 to 1,500 knights, and concentrated them at the Templar fortress of La Feve (al-Fule). For a full week the two armies skirmished without joining a decisive battle. Despite the systematic destruction of Frankish farms and...

On 7 September 1101, Baldwin I of Jerusalem met a Fatimid invasion army at Ramla, the strategic crossroads on the Jerusalem–Jaffa road. With only 260 knights and about 900 infantry — a force outnumbered perhaps tenfold — the king nonetheless ordered a ferocious mounted charge after his initial line buckled. Heavy casualties on both sides included some eighty Frankish knights, but the Fatimid commander Saad al-Daulah was killed in the fighting and his army broke and fled south. The victory secure...

On 17 May 1102, Baldwin I made the worst tactical mistake of his reign. Misjudging an incoming Fatimid army under the vizier's son Sharaf al-Ma'ali as a small raiding force, the king led only 200 knights — with no infantry support at all — directly into roughly 20,000 Egyptian troops at Ramla. The knights were enveloped and annihilated almost to a man. Stephen of Blois, who had returned to the East to atone for deserting the First Crusade, was among the dead; Baldwin himself escaped only by taki...

Three years after the catastrophe of 1102, Baldwin I returned to Ramla on 27 August 1105 with the lessons of combined-arms warfare fully internalised. With 500 knights and 2,000 infantry, he met a joint Fatimid–Damascene army that fielded a substantial corps of 1,300 Turkish horse archers supplied by the Burid dynasty — the first time a major coalition of Egyptian infantry and Syrian light cavalry had operated against the kingdom. The Crusader infantry absorbed the arrow harassment without break...

column7: Jordan River crossing, near Ateret Fortress
column15: King Baldwin IV had begun, in late 1178, to build a major castle — Chastellet — at Jacob's Ford on the Upper Jordan, in clear violation of an existing truce with Saladin. The site dominated the main crossing on the Damascus–Acre road and threatened to amputate Saladin's communications between Egypt and Syria. Saladin first tried to buy the project off; when Baldwin refused, the Ayyubid army arrived in late August 1179 with the half-finished castle still vulnerable. After five days of mining and ...

Two years after Saladin took Acre in the wake of Hattin, the freshly released King Guy of Lusignan arrived before its walls in August 1189 with a small force and laid siege to the city — only to be encircled in turn by Saladin's relief army. The resulting double siege, with Crusaders and Muslims facing each other across the walls and the surrounding plain, lasted twenty-two months. It was the central military event of the Third Crusade: tens of thousands of Frankish reinforcements arrived by sea...

column4: Fall of Acre; Mamluk siege of Acre
column15: The Mamluk sultan al-Ashraf Khalil arrived before Acre on 5 April 1291 with the largest siege army the medieval Levant had ever seen — contemporary chronicles claim a hundred and twenty thousand men and dozens of trebuchets, including two enormous machines named 'The Furious' and 'The Victorious'. Inside the walls, perhaps fifteen thousand defenders — Templars, Hospitallers, Teutonic Knights, the militias of the Italian quarters, and the household knights of King Henry II of Cyprus — held a near...

For more than half a century after the First Crusade the Fatimid fortress of Ascalon had remained a thorn in the kingdom's side, a permanent garrison from which raiding columns could reach the gates of Jerusalem. From 25 January to 22 August 1153, King Baldwin III mounted the largest siege of the kingdom's history, deploying enormous siege towers against the walls and a fleet of fifteen ships against seventy Fatimid vessels in the harbour. On 16 August the Fatimid garrison set fire to a Crusader...

The climactic moment of the First Crusade and one of the most consequential sieges in medieval history. After a gruelling two-year march from Constantinople, the surviving Crusader army — perhaps thirteen thousand strong, far from home and desperately short of water — arrived before the walls of Jerusalem in June 1099. The city's Fatimid garrison had poisoned the wells outside the walls and expelled all Christians. A first assault on 13 June failed. Only after Genoese ships landed timber at Jaff...

After the catastrophic destruction of the Crusader field army at Hattin on 4 July 1187, the cities and castles of the kingdom — stripped of their garrisons to feed the doomed march to Tiberias — fell to Saladin one after another over the summer. By September Saladin stood before the walls of Jerusalem itself. The defence was conducted by Balian of Ibelin, who had been permitted to enter the city under safe conduct to collect his wife. Balian raised every able-bodied man over fourteen and knighte...

Beneath the great Herodian enclosure of the Cave of the Patriarchs, the Crusaders built a fortified annex to serve as the castle of the lords of Hebron, the 'Castle of St Abraham'. The medieval town was one of the southernmost outposts of the kingdom. The Crusader additions are now embedded in the much longer architectural history of the Haram el-Khalil.

column4: Jabal ad-Duqq; Tahunat al-Hawa; Doctis; castellum Abrahamti
column7: Mount of Temptation, Jericho, West Bank
column15: A Templar castle established on the summit of Jabal Quruntul, the traditional Mount of Temptation above Jericho, reusing natural caves and cisterns. The site is now best known for the cliff-clinging Greek Orthodox Monastery of the Temptation, which was built centuries later on and around the earlier remains.

column4: Castellum Beleismum; Chastiau St Job; Khirbet Balama
column15: Khirbet Bal'ama — Castellum Beleismum, 'the castle of St Job' — stood at an important crossroads south of Jenin. A two-storey vaulted tower inside a rectangular enclosure protected a long vaulted tunnel leading down to a spring. The tunnel, partly excavated in recent years, is still visible.

Nablus — Neapolis to the Romans, Naples to the Crusaders — was a major royal town in the heart of Samaria. The battered base of a large tower, presumed to be part of the town's castle, still stands in the old city alongside other medieval fragments. Crusader Nablus was the scene of a 1120 ecclesiastical council which issued a set of influential legal canons.

column2: Qal'at ad-Damm / Good Samaritan / Cisterna Rubea
column3: Qal'at ad-Damm — Inn of the Good Samaritan
column4: Maldoim; Adumim; Castrum Dumi; Cisterna Rubea; Turris Rubea; Rouge Cisterne; Inn of the Good Samaritan
column7: Inn of the Good Samaritan, West Bank
column15: On the hill above the traditional Inn of the Good Samaritan, halfway between Jerusalem and Jericho, stand the ruins of a Templar road fortress known as Cisterna Rubea ('the Red Cistern') or Qal'at ad-Damm. It guarded pilgrims on the notoriously dangerous descent to Jericho and the Jordan.

Castellum Arnoldi, 'Arnold's Castle', stood at the entrance to the Ayalon Valley at Yalu, one of the classic invasion routes up to Jerusalem. It guarded the western flank of the kingdom's heartland. The medieval ruins survive today in the largely abandoned village area near Canada Park.

Al-Bira, next to Ramallah, was the Crusader 'new town' of Magna Mahumeria, a planned Frankish settlement attached to the Church of the Holy Sepulchre. At its lower end stood a small castle or tower with a barrel-vaulted basement. The parish church of Magna Mahumeria is still partially visible beneath later buildings in al-Bira.

column4: Spring of the Brigands/Templars; Birkat al-Dawiyya; Pool of the Templars
column15: 'The Spring of the Brigands' — called the Pool of the Templars in the sources — was a Templar-controlled water source and small road station on the Jerusalem-Nablus road. A groin-vaulted cistern still survives in the Wadi al-Haramiya area.

Bayt 'Ur al-Fauqa — biblical Upper Beth Horon — stood on the ancient pass from the coastal plain up to Jerusalem. A Frankish tower known simply as al-Burj ('the tower') marked the Crusader presence there; the village was granted to the Holy Sepulchre in the twelfth century.

Burj Bardawil — 'Baldwin's Tower' — is a small semi-fortified courtyard building in the central West Bank hills, with projecting turrets and barrel-vaulted ranges. Its name links it in popular memory to one of the first Crusader kings of Jerusalem, though the precise building history is uncertain.

A twelfth-century tower at Burj Beitin, identified by some with biblical Bethel. The tower sits inside a rectangular enclosure and was held at various times by ecclesiastical lords of the kingdom. Substantial walls survive on the hill above the modern village of Beitin.

Jericho in the Crusader period was more of a sugar-producing agricultural centre than a fortified stronghold. A square tower near the Russian compound may preserve a small Frankish presence in the oasis, but its attribution is debated. The wider Crusader footprint in the Jordan Valley was mainly economic.

A Frankish tower incorporating the fabric of an earlier church, on a hilltop in the southern West Bank. The tower had a barrel-vaulted basement and arrow-slits, and formed part of the scattered network of small strongpoints in the hills south of Hebron.

A two-storey Frankish tower with a barrel-vaulted basement and associated medieval buildings in the village of Qarawat Bani Hassan in the central West Bank. It represents the kind of small rural stronghold held by secondary vassals of the kingdom.

Al-Burj — 'the tower' — in the middle of what is now Ramallah preserves the surviving remains of a small medieval fortified building. The site is a reminder that Ramallah, like many of the hill-country villages, had a Frankish presence in the twelfth century.

Sinjil takes its name from the Crusader lord Raymond of Saint-Gilles, count of Toulouse and of Tripoli. A small Frankish tower and the surrounding village were registered as the estate of a named lord in 1175. The surviving base of the tower can still be seen in the village.

Ar-Ram, a village on the old Jerusalem-Nablus road, had a substantial Frankish tower with a barrel-vaulted basement and an internal stair leading up to the upper floor. It belonged to the network of small strongpoints that policed the main north-south route through the central hill country.

column4: An-Nasr Mosque; Mosque of Victory; Masjid an-Nasr
column15: The An-Nasr (Victory) Mosque in the heart of the Old City of Nablus stands on the site of a Crusader church, itself built over an earlier Byzantine basilica. The Crusaders, who governed the city as Naples in the twelfth century and held it as a royal demesne after King Baldwin II granted it to his daughter Princess Alice, raised the church on the central square of the Frankish quarter. After Saladin's reconquest in 1187 the building was converted into a mosque and given the name 'Victory' to com...

column4: Nabi Yahya Mosque; Sebastiya Cathedral; St John the Baptist's Cathedral
column15: Tradition placed the burial of John the Baptist, the prophets Elisha and Obadiah, and Empress Helena's relics of the Baptist all at Sebastia, on a hilltop above the road north of Nablus. The Crusaders rebuilt an earlier Byzantine church in the twelfth century into a vast Gothic-influenced basilica, second in size in the entire Holy Land only to the Holy Sepulchre itself. The cathedral served as the seat of a Latin bishop and an important pilgrimage destination. After Saladin's victory in 1187 it...

The core of the building is a 4th- and 6th-century Byzantine basilica over the grotto venerated as Christ's birthplace, but the Crusaders restored, decorated and embellished it extensively during their 12th-century rule. Between 1165 and 1169, wealthy pilgrims and the imperial dynasties of Byzantium and the Latin Kingdom jointly funded the addition of lavish wall mosaics and column paintings of saints; much of this Comnenian and Frankish decoration survives and was restored in the 2010s.

column4: Al-Jami' al-Kabir, Nablus; Nablus Great Mosque; Cathedral of the Holy Resurrection (Nablus); Church of the Passion (Nablus)
column15: The Great Mosque of Nablus — al-Jami' al-Kabir, the largest and oldest mosque of the Old City — occupies the shell of the Crusader cathedral of the Holy Resurrection (sometimes identified in twelfth-century sources as the Church of the Passion or the Church of St John). The Franks rebuilt an earlier Byzantine basilica into a major three-aisled Romanesque cathedral on this site in the 1160s, when Nablus served as a royal demesne and an occasional refuge for the court. After the city's fall to Sal...

column4: Nebi Samuel; Montjoie; Mons Gaudii; Mount of Joy; Tomb of Samuel
column15: Nabi Samwil — 'the prophet Samuel' — stands on the highest hilltop north of Jerusalem and is the traditional site of Samuel's tomb. The Crusaders knew it as Montjoie ('Mount of Joy') because it was here, according to tradition, that the knights of the First Crusade first caught sight of Jerusalem in June 1099. A Premonstratensian monastery and a fortified church were built on the summit in the twelfth century. After the loss of Jerusalem in 1187 the Franks briefly held Nabi Samwil as a forward o...

column4: Ayn al-Habis; Cave de Suet; Habis Jaldak; 'Ain Habis
column15: Ain al-Habis — known to the Crusaders as Cave de Suet — is one of the most unusual fortifications of the Latin Kingdom: a true cave castle, cut directly into the cliff face of the Yarmuk gorge in the southern Golan Heights. The complex consists of three storeys of natural and enlarged caves linked by internal staircases, with a spring inside the rock that made it almost impossible to starve out. Built or refortified in the twelfth century to control the rich grain country east of the Sea of Gali...

column4: Qal'at al-Subeiba; Qal'at Namrud; Mivtzar Nimrod; Subeibe
column15: Nimrod Fortress is the largest medieval castle in the region, a long, narrow stronghold strung along a rocky ridge at 800 metres elevation on the south-western slopes of Mount Hermon. It was begun in 1229 by al-Aziz 'Uthman, son of the Ayyubid sultan al-Adil, specifically to block a Crusader advance on Damascus through the Banias gap. The Mamluks later expanded it into one of the most elaborate fortresses of the thirteenth century, with huge rectangular towers, secret postern gates, a keep, and ...

Banias — Belinas to the Franks — lies at the foot of Mount Hermon where one of the main sources of the Jordan emerges. A strategically vital frontier town, it changed hands several times in the twelfth century between the kingdom and Damascus before being finally lost in 1164. Walls, gates and the base of a small castle survive in the archaeological park, alongside earlier Roman and Hellenistic remains.

Deir al-Balah — 'the monastery of the date palms' — is the site of the twelfth-century Crusader castle of Darum, built by King Amalric around 1170 on the desert road into Egypt. A four-tower quadrangular castle, it was besieged and rebuilt several times before Saladin finally destroyed it after Hattin. Almost nothing of the castle is visible today in the modern Gaza Strip town.

Originally a Byzantine church of St John converted to a mosque in early Islamic times and re-converted to a Crusader church in 1150 under the Templars. After Saladin's victory in 1187 the building reverted to a mosque — the Great Mosque of Gaza — but the Templars' Romanesque nave, pointed arches and handsome western portal remained intact and are still clearly visible today as an architectural palimpsest at the heart of the old city.

The forgotten catastrophe that sealed the fate of Crusader Palestine. By the 1240s the Crusader states had regained Jerusalem through diplomacy, but their position was fragile. In 1244 a horde of displaced Khwarezmian Turks — refugees from the Mongol conquests in Central Asia — swept through Syria and sacked Jerusalem, driving out its Christian inhabitants for the last time. The Crusaders allied with their former enemy, the Ayyubid rulers of Damascus and Homs, and together they marched to confro...

column4: Qal'at ar-Rabad; Ajlun Castle; Castle of 'Izz ad-Din Usama
column15: Ajloun is the great Ayyubid castle, built specifically as the answer to the Crusader fortresses across the Jordan. Saladin's general 'Izz ad-Din Usama, governor of the Ajloun region, began the work in 1184 to control the iron mines of the surrounding hills, to dominate the northern caravan road from Damascus to Egypt, and — above all — to block the Frankish lords of Belvoir and Kawkab from raiding into the rich agricultural land of the Sawad. A square inner keep with four corner towers was expan...

Perched dramatically on a long Jordanian plateau south of the Dead Sea, Kerak is one of the Levant's most formidable surviving medieval fortresses. Pagan the Butler began building it in the 1140s to anchor the Lordship of Oultrejordain and tax caravans on the King's Highway between Damascus and Egypt. The notorious Raynald of Châtillon made Kerak his base for raids on Muslim pilgrimage traffic, provoking Saladin's 1183 siege — interrupted, according to legend, only so that a noble wedding inside...

column4: Krak de Montreal; Mons Regalis; Shawbak
column15: Montreal — Mons Regalis, 'the royal mountain' — was founded by King Baldwin I in 1115 on an isolated conical hill in the southern Jordanian highlands. It served as the first seat of the Lordship of Oultrejordain before Kerak was built further north, and from here its lords taxed the caravan and pilgrimage traffic crossing between Syria, Egypt and the Hejaz. The castle withstood Saladin's armies from 1187 onward, finally surrendering in May 1189 after a nearly two-year blockade in which its garri...

The ancient acropolis of Rabbath-Ammon — classical Philadelphia — briefly served in the twelfth century as the seat of the small lordship of Ahamant, a sub-fief of Oultrejordain. The Franks are documented as holding the site but apparently reused the Byzantine and Umayyad fortifications on the Citadel Hill rather than building anew; archaeologists have yet to isolate a clearly Crusader-period structure. Ahamant slipped back into Muslim control before Hattin, and the name survives only in the lor...

column4: al-Wu'aira; al-Wu'ayra; Castellum Vallis Moysis; li Vaux Moysi (Valley of Moses)
column7: Wadi Musa, 1 km north of Petra entrance, Jordan
column15: A small spur castle sitting on a narrow ridge about a kilometre north of the entrance to Petra, Wu'ayra — Latin *Castellum Vallis Moysis*, 'Castle of the Valley of Moses' — was founded by Baldwin I as a forward outpost of Montreal, thirty-four kilometres to the north. From its 1,050-metre perch it watched the approaches to Wadi Musa and the trade and pilgrimage roads that skirted the Sharah plateau. More dependent on sheer cliffs than on heavy masonry, its most striking survival is a gatehouse h...

Consecrated in 1118 inside the courts of Kerak Castle, this is the largest surviving Crusader church in modern Jordan. Its architecture shows a striking collaboration between Latin architects and local Arab builders: Frankish ribbed vaults and pointed arches sit over locally-dressed ashlar walls, producing a distinctive hybrid of Occitan and Moabite masonry.

column4: Île de Graye; Ile de Graye; Jazirat Fara'un; Gezirat Faraun; Coral Island; Salah El-Din Castle; Saladin's Castle; Pharaoh's Island
column7: Pharaoh's Island (Jazirat Fara'un), Gulf of Aqaba, Egypt
column15: A small granite island at the northern head of the Gulf of Aqaba carries the southernmost outwork associated with the Kingdom of Jerusalem. Tradition — preserved by William of Tyre — credits King Baldwin I with building a fortress here around 1116 to tax and threaten the Red Sea pilgrim traffic; modern scholarship is cautious, since documented Frankish garrison records are thin. In 1170 Saladin's engineers seized the island and dramatically enlarged the defences, giving the castle its modern Ara...

column4: Citadel of Saladin; Qal'at al-Jabal; Salah ad-Din Citadel
column15: Saladin began the Cairo Citadel in 1176 on a spur of the Mokattam hills overlooking the city. It was a deliberate strategic move: a single fortified enclosure unifying the Fatimid palace city of al-Qahira with the older settlement of al-Fustat, and giving the new Ayyubid sultanate a defensible command centre from which to co-ordinate the wars against the Crusader states. From this citadel Saladin and his heirs directed the campaigns that broke the Kingdom of Jerusalem at Hattin in 1187 and the d...

Louis IX of France's Seventh Crusade had landed at Damietta in June 1249 and then moved up the eastern arm of the Nile delta toward Cairo. On 8 February 1250, the Crusader vanguard under the king's brother Robert d'Artois forded the Bahr el-Saghir branch of the Nile and surprised an Ayyubid camp at Gideila; flushed with success, Robert ignored orders to wait for the main army and pursued the fleeing Egyptians into the narrow streets of the town of al-Mansurah itself. There the elite Bahri Mamluk...

column4: Sur; Sour; Ṣūr; Tyr; Tyrus; Seigneury of Tyre
column15: One of the four great ports of the Kingdom of Jerusalem, usually held as part of the royal domain. Besieged for five and a half months by a combined Crusader–Venetian force in 1124 — the Venetian fleet blockading the harbour — and taken on 7 July that year. After the catastrophe at the Horns of Hattin in 1187, Tyre was the only major port of the kingdom to hold out against Saladin, defended heroically by Conrad of Montferrat, whose two defensive victories gave the Third Crusade its base of opera...

One of the most striking examples of Crusader military architecture in the Levant, perched on a sheer rock over the Litani. Fulk of Jerusalem captured the site in 1139; the Franks built a roughly triangular fortress (150 × 100 m) in two wards with a 12 × 12 m keep — notable for its Syrian-style ground-floor entrance rather than the usual European first-floor door. Saladin took it after a long siege in 1189–90, during which the Frankish lord Reynald of Sidon famously stalled surrender negotiation...

Seat of the Genoese Embriaco family, who ruled Gibelet as naval magnates from 1100 until the late thirteenth century. A hybrid castrum–turris plan: roughly square curtain walls with four corner towers enclosing a massive central donjon, surrounded by a moat and faced with reused Roman columns. Saladin took it in 1188 after Hattin and partially dismantled it in 1190; the Franks recovered Byblos in 1197 and rebuilt the defences, which repulsed a Cypriot naval assault as late as 1369.

column4: Chqif Tayroun; Fortress of Niha; Qal'at Niha
column15: Troglodyte fortress carved into a sheer 100-metre cliff over the Bisri and Aray valleys, guarding the road between Sidon and the Beqaa. First mentioned in 975 CE and held by Druze leaders in the early twelfth century, it was integrated into the Lordship of Sidon around 1170. Rock-cut chambers, deep silos, and a spring-fed cistern system allowed it to withstand long sieges. Sold to the Teutonic Knights by Julian of Sidon in 1256; lost in 1261; rebuilt by Baibars in 1270.

column4: Château du Mont-Pèlerin; Castellum Montis Peregrini; Qal'at Sān Jīl; Citadel of Tripoli
column15: Headquarters of the County of Tripoli for a century and a half, built from 1102 onward by Raymond IV of Toulouse on Mount Pilgrim two miles from the old city to blockade Tripoli. The city finally fell to the Crusaders in July 1109. Sultan Qalawun captured it in 1289, burning the Mont Pèlerin quarter; the hilltop lay abandoned for eighteen years before the Mamluks regarrisoned it. Very little original Frankish masonry survives — the present citadel is largely a nineteenth-century Ottoman restorat...

Sprawling 17,000-m² Crusader castle with 12-metre walls, seven towers, 365 wells, four springs, and extensive subterranean cellars and stables — one of the largest surviving plan castles of southern Lebanon. Tradition associates its renovation with a French commander called Miron, hence the alternate name 'Maron Castle'. Levelled by Sultan Qalawun in 1289 and partially restored in the mid-eighteenth century by Sheikh Abbas al-Nassar al-Waeli.

column4: Hisn Ibn Akkar; Qal'at 'Akkar; Akkar el-Atika
column15: Spur castle on a 700-metre ridge between two ravines of the Nahr Akkar, guarding the Homs Gap road 27 km south of Krak des Chevaliers. Ceded by the Burids in 1109, held by the Puylaurens family, then assigned to the Hospitallers by King Amalric I after the 1170 earthquake. Captured by Nur ad-Din in 1167 and recovered; finally taken by Sultan Baibars on 11 May 1271, immediately after the fall of Krak des Chevaliers.

Peninsular fief of the Counts of Tripoli, built on a Phoenician site where the Crusaders executed one of the great engineering feats of the Middle Ages: a sea-level moat cut over 100 yards through living rock, severing the citadel from the mainland. The Lords of Nephin became notorious robber barons preying on Jerusalem–Tripoli traffic. Sultan Qalawun demolished the castle so thoroughly in 1289 that only the rock-cut moat survives.

column4: Qal'at al-Bahr; Château de la Mer de Sidon
column15: Marvel of maritime engineering built in 1228 by the Knights Hospitaller on a small rocky island connected to Sidon's port by an 80-metre causeway. The Crusaders deliberately chose an islet that had housed an ancient Phoenician temple to Melqart, reusing the submerged foundations. The lower phase of the east tower is Frankish; the Mamluks partially destroyed and then rebuilt the upper tower and extended the causeway after taking Sidon.

Twelfth-century Crusader castle above the Batroun district with deep pre-Crusader history — Maronite Patriarch St John Maron took refuge here in 685 CE. Built in three phases over a Roman-period necropolis as a tower-and-bailey fortress: central donjon on a rock podium, curtain walls, four corner towers, a rock-cut ditch and a barbican at the entrance. It later passed to the Khazin family; damaged in the 1630 earthquake and restored the following year.

Spur castle on a steep Bronze Age tell at 725 metres, built in 1106 by Hugh of Fauquembergues, Prince of Galilee, to pressure the still-Muslim port of Tyre. Originally defended by twelve rectangular towers and a massive southern donjon; seat of the Humphrey family, Lords of Toron. Fell to Saladin after Hattin in 1187; the Ayyubid sultan al-Mu'azzam secretly dismantled it in 1219 to deny it to the Fifth Crusade. Frederick II recovered it diplomatically in 1229, and it resisted a Khwarezmian attac...

Coastal town captured in 1104 and held as the Lordship of Botrun until the Mamluk conquest of 1289, when Sultan Qalawun destroyed the citadel so completely that its exact site has been lost. The lordship produced a long line of counts but left no upstanding Frankish fortifications; the picturesque stretch of Phoenician sea-wall sometimes pointed out in the modern town predates the Crusaders entirely.

Crusader-built coastal fortification guarding the port of Beirut, held by the Ibelin family as the Lordship of Beirut and a crucial defensive node for the central Levant. Demolished in the late nineteenth century during Ottoman urban expansion; only sparse archaeological remains survive beneath the modern city centre.

column4: Château de Hasbaya; Emir Shahib's Castle
column15: Frankish square tower guarding the approaches to Mount Hermon, held by a Crusader identified in local tradition as Count Oran. Lost to the Chehab dynasty in 1171 after fierce fighting with the Druze, themselves formidable warriors resisting the Frankish penetration of the mountains. The Chehabs expanded the Crusader tower into a 65-room palatial complex decorated with Renaissance wall paintings, occupying it as a family seat for over eight centuries.

column4: Qalaat al Muizz; Sidon Land Castle; Château Saint-Louis
column15: Landward counterpart to the Sidon Sea Castle, perched on Sidon's ancient acropolis over a tenth-century Fatimid citadel, itself built on demolished classical structures. The site was devastated by an Ayyubid attack in 1249, and completely reconstructed in 1254 by French Crusaders during Louis IX's occupation of the city. Together with the Sea Castle it made Sidon one of the most imposing Frankish strongholds on the coast until it was sacked by the Mongols under Kitbuqa in 1260.

Chokepoint fortress on the roads from Tripoli to Tartus and Homs. Raymond of Toulouse besieged it unsuccessfully for three months in 1099 during the First Crusade; William II Jordan finally took it in 1108 and incorporated it into the County of Tripoli. The castle resisted Nur ad-Din in 1167 and 1171 before falling to Baibars in 1265–66.

column4: Qalaat Abou El Hassan; Belhasem; Belhacem Fortress
column15: 80-metre-high rocky outcrop (100 × 30 m) near Sfariyeh in the Jezzine region, first mentioned in Frankish chronicles in 1128 as a rear-guard defence for Sidon against attacks from the eastern mountains. Its signature survival is a semi-circular two-story tower-gate built with large rusticated bossage blocks — the same masonry signature seen at Beaufort. Fell to Saladin in September 1187 after Hattin and became an Ayyubid frontier post.

Square castrum with flanking towers and vaulted halls on a low knoll 2 km from the Akkar coast — architecturally a classic plain- country military-order fortress. Granted to the Knights Hospitaller by Count Pons of Tripoli in 1127. Dismantled by Saladin's brother al-Adil in 1207 and permanently ruined by Baibars in summer 1266 as a prelude to the strangulation of Tripoli. Today only fragmentary remains stand.

Twelfth-century watchtower (turris) on the Damascus–Tyre road near Chaqra, likely housing a single knight and his household as a physical marker of lordly authority within the Lordship of Toron. French archaeological missions in 2011–2016 showed the Frankish foundations were radically refortified by Shia lords in the seventeenth century; their additions are unusual in the Near East for including domestic fireplaces but no firearm loopholes.

Conical-hill castle in Shamaa built by Baldwin I in 1116 (some sources 1124) to command the coastal approach to Tyre. Its walls and flanking towers gave sweeping views down the littoral as far as Naqoura, allowing early warning of any maritime or overland threat against the greatest of the northern Galilean ports.

Founded in 1129 as the metropolitan cathedral of Tyre, this was one of the largest and most prestigious churches of the Latin Kingdom of Jerusalem. After the loss of Jerusalem in 1187 it became the principal coronation site of the kings of the 'Second Kingdom', and hosted a number of royal marriages. Tradition — contested among the chronicles — holds that the bones of Holy Roman Emperor Frederick Barbarossa, who drowned in the River Saleph on his way to the Third Crusade, were interred here in 1...

column2: Our Lady of the Wind / Saydet el-Rih (Anfeh)
column3: Our Lady of the Wind / Saydet el-Rih (Anfeh)
column7: Our Lady of the Wind / Saydet el-Rih (Anfeh)
column15: Small church on the rocky Anfeh peninsula, originally a Byzantine chapel that the Crusaders rebuilt and expanded by adding a western narthex and a northern chamber. It is one of the very few churches in Lebanon to retain medieval frescoes from the Crusader era, depicting saints, angels and biblical scenes including the sacrifice of Isaac.

Cathedral of Saint John the Baptist built by the Crusaders in 1115 in the Romanesque style just inside the walls of Gibelet (modern Byblos). Its most distinctive feature is an open-air domed baptistery on the north side dating to the original 12th-century construction. Earthquakes reduced it to disrepair in later centuries; in 1764 the building was granted to the Lebanese Maronite Order and rededicated to St Jean-Marc.

On 10 June 1179, Baldwin IV intercepted a Saladin-led raiding column returning to Damascus through the Marj Ayyun valley in what is now southern Lebanon. The Crusader vanguard caught the Ayyubid baggage train and inflicted heavy losses, but a concentrated counter-attack by Saladin's reserves caught the Franks scattered across the valley floor. The Crusader force was driven back in disorder; the master of the Templars, Odo of St Amand, was captured along with Hugh of Tiberias and several hundred ...

From 19 October to 5 December 1110, Baldwin I besieged the Fatimid-held coastal city of Sidon with the assistance of the Doge of Venice Ordelafo Faliero and a Norwegian fleet of sixty ships under King Sigurd I, who had taken the cross and travelled by sea via Spain and Sicily to fight in the Holy Land. The combined land-and-sea operation cut the city off completely; the garrison surrendered on terms in early December. The capture of Sidon established the Lordship of Sidon as one of the great fie...

Tyre was the last great Fatimid port on the Levantine coast and the most heavily fortified, jutting out into the Mediterranean on an island linked to the mainland only by a narrow causeway. The siege began in February 1124 under the Patriarch Warmund of Picquigny and the new regent William of Bures — the previous regent, Eustace Grenier, having died in December 1123 — with critical naval support from a Venetian fleet bound by the Pactum Warmundi, a treaty granting Venice extensive trading and te...

column4: Crac des Chevaliers; Hisn al-Akrad; Castle of the Kurds; Qal'at al-Hisn
column15: The greatest of the Hospitaller castles and the most complete Crusader fortress to survive anywhere in the world. The Knights Hospitaller received the site in 1142 from Raymond II of Tripoli and rebuilt the earlier Kurdish fort into a colossal concentric stronghold of two circuits of walls, with a sloping talus, vaulted halls, a great Gothic loggia, and quarters for a garrison of perhaps two thousand men. Saladin probed it in 1188 and decided it was not worth besieging. It survived almost every ...

Margat — 'the watchtower' — is the second great Hospitaller castle of the Syrian coast, built of black basalt on a 360-metre volcanic spur overlooking the sea between Latakia and Tartus. The Mazoir family fortified the site in 1117 and sold it to the Hospitallers in 1186, who rebuilt it on a vast scale: a massive double curtain wall, a circular keep, a Gothic chapel, and barracks for a thousand-strong garrison with provisions enough for a five-year siege. After the fall of Krak in 1271 it became...

column4: Saladin's Castle; Sahyun; Qal'at Salah ad-Din; Castle of Saone
column15: Saone occupies a long mountain ridge between two deep ravines about thirty kilometres east of Latakia, in territory belonging to the Principality of Antioch. The Crusaders refortified an earlier Byzantine site in the early twelfth century, cutting a spectacular rock-hewn ditch 28 metres deep through the saddle of the spur and leaving a single needle of living rock to support the drawbridge — one of the most dramatic engineering feats of the medieval Mediterranean. Saladin captured the castle in ...

column2: Cathedral of Our Lady of Tortosa
column3: Cathedral of Our Lady of Tortosa
column4: Tortosa Cathedral; Notre-Dame de Tortose; Cathedral of Tartus
column15: Captured by the Franks in 1099, Tortosa (modern Tartus on the Syrian coast) hosted what is widely considered the best-preserved Crusader religious building anywhere in the Levant. The Knights Templar took the city in 1152 and rebuilt the cathedral around an older shrine that pilgrims believed had been consecrated by Saint Peter. Anticipating Mamluk attacks in the 1260s, the Templars added defensive sacristies, machicolated buttresses, and arrow-loops directly into the cathedral fabric — a rare e...

column4: Tortosa Citadel; Crusader Castle of Tartus
column15: The largest and most heavily fortified of the County of Tripoli's coastal strongholds. Crusaders first besieged the city in 1099 but lost it shortly after; Raymond IV of Toulouse retook it in February 1102 after a fortnight's siege. Around 1165 the Knights Templar assumed guardianship and rebuilt the castle on a grand scale, with two concentric defensive walls, eleven towers, a lancet-windowed chapel, and a postern gate that allowed the harbour to resupply the garrison directly. The citadel with...

Following the catastrophic Frankish defeat at the Field of Blood in 1119, the Principality of Antioch was hanging by a thread, and Baldwin II of Jerusalem assumed the regency of the northern Crusader states to prevent their collapse. On 11 June 1125, near the small town of Azaz north of Aleppo, Baldwin assembled a coalition of 1,100 knights and 2,000 infantry drawn from Jerusalem, Antioch, Edessa, and Tripoli. They faced a Seljuk-Artuqid army of perhaps 15,000 under Aq-Sunqur il-Bursuqi and Togh...

On 12 August 1164, Nur ad-Din invested the strategic Frankish fortress of Harim (Harenc), which guarded the eastern approaches to Antioch. A combined relief army from the County of Tripoli, the Principality of Antioch, the Byzantine Empire, and Cilician Armenia marched to break the siege but was lured into a trap and annihilated. The casualties read like a roll-call of the Frankish north: Bohemond III of Antioch, Raymond III of Tripoli, Hugh VIII of Lusignan, and the Byzantine governor Constanti...

column4: Battle of Ard al-Hatim; Fons Muratus
column15: On 29 June 1149, Nur ad-Din — the Zengid atabeg whose career would re-unite Muslim Syria — destroyed the field army of the Principality of Antioch at Inab, near the village of Ard al-Hatim. Prince Raymond of Poitiers had ridden out with 4,000 cavalry and 1,000 infantry, accompanied by Ali ibn Wafa and a contingent of Nizari Assassins. Nur ad-Din encircled them with 6,000 men and wiped the army out almost to the last man; Raymond and Ali ibn Wafa were both killed and Raymond's skull was sent in a...

column4: Mamluk capture of Krak; Baybars at Krak
column7: Krak des Chevaliers, Homs Gap, Syria
column15: The Mamluk sultan Baybars arrived before Krak des Chevaliers in late February 1271 with the explicit aim of breaking the back of the Hospitaller presence in northern Syria. After a month of bombardment with trebuchets and the methodical mining of the great southern tower, his sappers broke through the outer wall on 29 March. The garrison, perhaps three hundred knights and sergeants, retreated to the inner enceinte and held out for another five weeks. On 8 April, Baybars produced a forged letter ...

column2: Church of St. George, Deir al-Asad
column3: Church of St. George, Deir al-Asad
column4: Saint Jorge Labane; Abbey Church of St George (Labeyne)
column15: The remains of a 12th-century Crusader church stand in the shared centre of the twin villages of Deir al-Asad and Bi'ina, the medieval caput of the fief of St George de la Beyne. Denys Pringle identified the building as an abbey church of St George, and Victor Guérin, visiting in 1875, recorded that it had three naves and three apses pierced by narrow windows, consistent with a Carthusian plan. The village took its modern Arabic name — "the lion's monastery" — from the conspicuous Frankish ruins...

column4: Fifth Crusade siege of Damietta; Siege of Dumyat
column15: In May 1218 the vanguard of the Fifth Crusade sailed into the eastern mouth of the Nile and dropped anchor opposite Damietta — the Ayyubid port that controlled the approach to Cairo. The city was protected by a great chain-tower standing in midstream and a second iron chain anchored to the eastern bank.
